abstract:
During the fourth Fire Lab at Missoula Experiment (FLAME-4), we deployed a high-resolution proton-transfer-reaction time-of-flight mass spectrometer (PTR-TOF-MS) to characterize gas-phase emissions from an extensive range of biomass fuel types, including peat, agricultural residues, and domestic cooking fires. To quantify the non-methane organic compounds (NMOCs) within these complex mixtures, we employed a hybrid calibration approach utilizing both gas standards and composition-sensitive, mass-dependent sensitivity curves. By integrating these measurements with Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, we successfully identified—either positively or tentatively—approximately 80–96% of the total detected NMOC mass across major fuel types, including several high-molecular-weight species.

We present a comprehensive suite of new emission factors (EFs) for globally significant biomass fuels, highlighting numerous compounds that are rarely or previously unrecorded. These include critical secondary organic aerosol precursors such as furans, phenolic compounds, and aromatic hydrocarbons. Our analysis reveals that oxygenated NMOCs constitute the largest fraction of emissions across all fuel categories. Furthermore, a comparison of cooking technologies demonstrates that traditional three-stone fires produce significantly higher EFs than more advanced stove designs. Finally, we report the detection of several understudied nitrogen-containing organic compounds; these species accounted for up to 8.7% of the fuel nitrogen and may contribute substantially to new particle formation in biomass smoke plumes.
